coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield nitrogen doped carbon dots for Ag+ sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, YingTe
Wu, RongRong
Zhang, YuanYuan
Cheng, SiRong
Zhang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with green fluorescence is developed by the hydrothermal reaction with o-aminophenol and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, and the quantum yield (QY) is 38.4%. The NCDs are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The results show that NCDs have good fluorescence properties and stability, and the NCDs are further utilized as a sensor for Ag+ via the static quenching effect (SQE) (λex=430 nm, λem=510 nm). The linear range of Ag+ is 30–210 µmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.7 µmol/L (S/N = 3). On this basis, we quantitatively detect Ag+ in real samples. Furthermore, due to the excellent fluorescence performance, the NCDs are found to be an effective tool for Ag+ sensing in bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ag+
Nitrogen doping
Fluorescence sensors
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real samples
Fluorescent bio-imaging of the NCDs for Ag+ sensing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Health Commission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported financially by Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Health Commission (Grant No. 2020TD14).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis and application of the NCDs.
(a) The TEM images; (b) size distribution; (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) XRD pattern of the NCDs.
(a) XPS full scan spectra; High-resolution (b) C1S; (c) N1S and (d) O1S spectra of the NCDs.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectra (black), fluorescence excitation spectra (blue line) and fluorescence emission spectra (red line) of NCDs. Inset: photos of NCDs solution under natural light (left) and ultraviolet light (365 nm) (right); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs at excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 470 nm; (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates; (d) Excitation-emission matrix of NCDs at excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 470 nm.
The effect of (a) pH; (b) ionic strength and (c) storage times on the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs.
The influences of different (a) metal ions and (b) organics on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs.
The effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs and Ag+ system.
(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs after adding various concentrations of Ag+ (0–320 µmol/L); (b) Relationship between F0-F and the concentration of Ag+(λex=430 nm).
(a) The fluorescence decay curve of the NCDs in the absence and presence of Ag+; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag+, NCDs and NCDs/Ag+, respectively.
Without Ag+ and with Ag+ as contrast, confocal fluorescence imaging of oocysts co-incubated with NCDs.
Comparison of the reported methods of Ag+ detection.
Application of Ag+ detection in real samples (n = 4).

head.ce:title: High quantum yield nitrogen doped carbon dots for Ag+ sensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with green fluorescence is developed by the hydrothermal reaction with o-aminophenol and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, and the quantum yield (QY) is 38.4%. The NCDs are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The results show that NCDs have good fluorescence properties and stability, and the NCDs are further utilized as a sensor for Ag+ via the static quenching effect (SQE) (λex=430 nm, λem=510 nm). The linear range of Ag+ is 30–210 µmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.7 µmol/L (S/N = 3). On this basis, we quantitatively detect Ag+ in real samples. Furthermore, due to the excellent fluorescence performance, the NCDs are found to be an effective tool for Ag+ sensing in bioimaging.

Herein, green-emission fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are developed by the hydrothermal reaction of o-aminophenol and ethylenediamine, and the quantum yield (QY) is 38.4%. The NCDs are utilized as a sensor for Ag+, and the linear range is 30–210 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.7 µM (S/N = 3). On the other hand, the NCDs are further applied to biological imaging, constructing the fluorescence analysis method of Ag+ in biological samples.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For the broad spectrum antibacterial property within a certain concentration range, sliver ion (Ag+) is widely used in daily supplies, water purification and pharmaceutical areas [1–5]. However, excessive usage of Ag+ has threatened human health and there is currently no effective mechanism to metabolize Ag+ from the human body to alleviate poisoning [6–9]. In consideration of strictly controlling over the concentration, it is meaningful for human health to sensitively detect Ag+. At present, the commonly used methods for Ag+ detection include electrochemical method, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10–12]. By contrast, these methods are cumbersome and subject to many limitations in practical use for detecting Ag+. Hence, in regard to sensing Ag+ sensitively and selectively by an economic and feasible method, there are still much left for improvement.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging nanomaterial that has drawn significant attention owing to its low cost, easy preparation, strong photoluminescence, and good photostability in recent years [13–16]. However, the quantum yield (QY) of most CDs is low, which reduces the sensitivity and practical application. For example, Li et al. synthesize CDs by one-step hydrothermal treatment of tetrahydrofuran (THF) to selectively detect Ag+ with the QY of 11.5% [17]. In order to improve the QY, many researchers try to optimize the reaction conditions. Heteroatom (N, S, P, B) doping methods are an effective way to enhance the fluorescence intensity and increase the QY of CDs [18]. Al-Qahtani et al. use NH4OH as the precursor to synthesize NCDs with the quantum yield of 22.51% via one-pot hydrothermal carbonization in an aquatic medium [19].

Inspired by this, using o-aminophenol (C6H7NO) and ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) as carbon and nitrogen sources, green luminescent N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with the QY of 38.4% are synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction. The prepared NCDs exhibit good water solubility, stability, and biocompatibility. Notably, the NCDs can detect Ag+ sensitively and selectively, with the liner range in 30–210 µmol/L and LOD of 4.7 µmol/L. Furthermore, owing to its biocompatible and optical properties of NCDs, it has further been applied in bioimaging with satisfactory results. As a result, the prepared NCDs show promising applications in detecting Ag+ of real samples and bioimaging of oocysts cells (Scheme 1 ).

O-aminophenol (C6H7NO) and ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) are purchased from Beijing Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Rhodamine 6 G is brought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All the above reagents are of analytical grade without further purification. The lake water comes from Lingde Lake of Shanxi University. The oocysts cells are provided by the Institute of Biomedical Sciences, Shanxi University.

Using JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of NCDs is obtained. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra is scanned by UV-2450 Ultraviolet Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the fluorescence spectra is detected with RF-5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). D8 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) is used to collect the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NCDs. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are recorded by Nicolet iS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer AXIS-ULTRADLD (Kratos, U.K.). FLS920 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, U.K.) is used to measure the fluorescence lifetime. The confocal fluorescence imaging is recorded by LSM880+ Airyscan Laser Confocal Microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

In the experiment, o-aminophenol (m = 0.4368 g) and ethylenediamine (V = 20 mL) are mixed with ultrapure water (V = 30 mL). After the solid powder is completely dissolved, the solution is transferred to a high-pressure reactor with polytetrafluoroethylene lining and heated continuously for 10 h at 200 °C. Subsequently, the cooled product is centrifuged (8000 rpm) for 20 min and dialyzed (MWCO=1000 Da) for 24 h to obtain the NCDs solution. After drying with a vacuum freeze-dryer, the NCDs powder are obtained and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

Using Rhodamine 6 G (QY=0.94) in water as a reference, the QY of NCDs is obtained. The QY is calculated according to formula (1):
(1)
Y C
=
Y R
(
I C
I R
)
(
A R
A C
)
(
η C 2
η R 2
)

Here, C and R refer to the NCDs and Rhodamine 6 G. I and Y are the area integral of the emission spectra (λex=430 nm) and QY. A and η represent the UV–vis absorbance and refractive index of the solution, respectively [20].

Typically, NCDs are mixed with ultrapure water as an initial solution (C = 0.47 g/L V = 2 mL). Then, different concentrations of Ag+ (V = 20 μL) are added to it. Ag+ and NCDs are allowed to fully react for 3 min, and their fluorescence emission spectra are measured (λex=430 nm). The selectivity of NCDs to Ag+ is investigated by adding potential interfering substances (C = 0.01 mol/L, V = 20 μL) into NCDs solution (C = 0.47 g/L, V = 2 mL). The above fluorescence detection is carried out at room temperature.

The experimental steps for oocysts cell imaging are as follows: at room temperature, oocysts solution (V = 10 μL) and NCDs solution (C = 0.47 g/L, V = 10 μL) are incubated on a glass slide for 20 min. Subsequently, the cells are washed by ultrapure water to remove the excess NCDs solution that could not enter the oocysts, and the fluorescence images are observed by confocal laser microscope (λex=430 nm). Finally, Ag+ solution (C = 0.01 mol/L) is added to the oocysts cells and incubated for 10 min to observe the change of the fluorescence intensity.

TEM is used to observe the nanostructure, morphology and particle size distribution of the synthesized NCDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, NCDs exhibit a micro spherical structure and dispersion in solution, with an average diameter of about 3.25 nm. The functional groups of NCDs surfaces are characterized by the FT-IR (Fig. 1c). The wide band located at 3344 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The stretching vibration of CH is located at 2939 cm−1, and the stretching vibrations of aromatic hydrocarbons C = C are located at 1616 cm−1 and 1514 cm−1. The absorption peak located at 1342 cm−1 confirms the existence of CH, and the absorption bands located at 1277 cm−1 and 1225 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and CN. The band at 748 cm−1 is attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibration of the benzene ring. The above analysis shows that NCDs contain many hydrophilic groups. As indicated in XRD (Fig. 1d), the broad diffraction peak is at 20.9°, demonstrating the presence of amorphous structure on NCDs.

The relative content of C, N and O of the NCDs are 58.72%, 28.03% and 13.25% (Fig. 2 a), shown with the three main peaks at 285.2 eV (C1s), 399.4 eV (N1s) and 531.6 eV (O1s)[21]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) demonstrates three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV and 287.5 eV, which is ascribed to CC/C = C, CN and C = O functional groups, respectively. The two peaks of CN and NH are located at 399.3 eV and 400.4 eV in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c). In the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), the two peaks of 531.09 eV and 532.5 eV could relate to the CO and COH functional groups, respectively [21,22]. This result indicates that a large number of hydrophilic groups exist on the surface of NCDs, and the N element is successfully doped into the CDs, which is consistent with the results of FT-IR.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs exhibit the intense absorption peaks at 240 nm, which attributes to the π-π* of C = C, and the peak at 295 nm is owing to the n-π* of C = O [23]. Besides, the optimal excitation wavelength is 430 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength is 510 nm. The inset shows that NCDs are pale yellow solution, while it displays bright green fluorescence under UV light (λ=365 nm). When λex changes from 320 nm to 470 nm, λem undergoes a slight red shift (from 500 nm to 520 nm), demonstrating that NCDs have excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 3b) [24]. The chromaticity diagram (CIE) (Fig. 3c) shows the green luminescence properties of NCDs. As can be seen from Fig. 3d, the excitation-emission matrix further demonstrates that the optimal excitation wavelength is 430 nm, and the emission peak is 510 nm, which is consistent with Fig. 3a. The QY of NCDs is calculated as 38.4% (λex=430 nm), using Rhodamine 6 G as a reference.

Considering the potential application of the NCDs, the stability is further investigated. Fig. 4 a illustrates that there are no obvious changes in fluorescence intensity in the range of pH from 2 to 12, implying that the effect of pH can be ignored, which makes NCDs a promising material for cellular imaging. When NaCl solution with different concentrations (0–0.826 mol/L) are added to the NCDs (Fig. 4b), the fluorescence intensity of NCDs changes slightly. Especially at a higher ionic intensity, NCDs can still maintain the original fluorescence intensity. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs remains constant within 10 weeks, demonstrating that the NCDs are relatively stable (Fig. 4c). The NCDs powder are stored in the fridge at 4 °C. When the powder is dispersed in ultrapure water, there is no aggregation phenomenon.

To demonstrate the selectivity of the NCDs for Ag+, different ions (Ag+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Li+, Al3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Hg2+) and small molecules (p-nitrophenol, hydroquinone, acetone, citric acid, urea, benzoic acid, 2,4-dinitrophenol, picric acid, ethylenediamine, nitrobenzene, 2,4-dichlorophenol, ammonium nitrate, o-nitrotoluene, m-nitrobenzoic acid, o-nitrobenzoic acid, m-hydroxybenzoic acid, salicylic acid) are measured. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, only Ag+ induces obvious fluorescence quenching, while the changes in fluorescence intensity caused by other species are negligible, suggesting that the NCDs can be used to detect Ag+ selectively.

When the NCDs are mixed with Ag+, the influence of reaction time is studied. After Ag+ is added, the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly, and remains stable after 3 min (Fig. 6 ). Consequently, 3 min is selected as the optimal reaction time for NCDs/Ag+ system.

Fig. 7 a shows the changes of fluorescence spectra after adding different concentrations (0–320 µmol/L) of Ag+. With the increase of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity decreases gradually and almost completely quenches in the end. What's more, the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs exhibits good linear relationships. A linear equation (F0-F = 2.829C–25.492) can be obtained within a range of 30–210 μM (R2=0.995), and the corresponding LOD is 4.7 µmol/L (S/N = 3) (Fig. 7b). Table 1 compares this method with the reported results. It is worth mentioning that this method has a relatively wider linear range.

To investigate the possible quenching mechanism between NCDs and Ag+, corresponding fluorescence lifetimes are investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 a, there are no obvious changes in the average lifetime for the NCDs before and after the addition of Ag+, demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching between NCDs and Ag+ may belong to the static quenching effect (SQE) [30]. In addition, from the UV–vis spectra in Fig. 8b, the NCDs have a characteristic absorption peak at 295 nm, after adding Ag+, new absorption bands appear at 343 and 426 nm. This demonstrates that new complexes form between NCDs and Ag+. Combining with Ag+ induces the fluorescence quenching but fluorescence lifetime has no changes, the NCDs/Ag+ complexes are deduced to be non-fluorescent complexes. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of the NCDs/Ag+ system is launched by non-fluorescent ground state complexes formation [30]. This phenomenon further implies that the quenching mechanism of Ag+ induced NCDs belongs to the SQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the analytical application of the proposed fluorescence quantum dot into consideration, Ag+ are detected in real samples (tap water and lake water). By standard addition method, four different concentrations (0 µmol/L, 35 µmol/L, 40 µmol/L and 45 µmol/L) of samples are studied. As shown in Table 2 , the relative standard deviations (RSD) (n = 4) are below 3%, the recovery rates are 98.71%−102.55% in tap water, and 98.22%−101.45% in lake water. These results indicate that the NCDs can be used as the fluorescent probes for the detection of Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent bio-imaging of the NCDs for Ag+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Under confocal laser microscopy, bio-imaging of the NCDs for Ag+ sensing on living oocysts cells are performed. From Fig. 9 , when oocysts cells is incubated with NCDs at room temperature for 20 min, the morphology is well maintained and accompanied by a bright green fluorescence emission (λex=405 nm). The oocysts cells treated with NCDs have not morphological changes substantially, revealing that the NCDs have low cytotoxicity to oocysts cells. In addition, adding Ag+ to the system and irradiating with light (λex=405 nm), the morphology of oocysts remains in good shape, while the green fluorescence emission is weakened and almost invisible finally. Thus, Ag+ induced NCDs fluorescence quenching can be performed in vivo in the oocysts. The results manifest that this work successfully constructed a fluorescence analysis method of NCDs for Ag+ sensing in biological samples.

Overall, NCDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction using o-aminophenol and ethylenediamine as raw materials, with the QY of 38.4%, as well as excitation wavelength dependence and good stability. Meanwhile, an sensitively and selectively sensor for Ag+ is developed, with the LOD of 4.7 µmol/L in the range of 30–210 µmol/L. The established sensor is adopted to detect Ag+ in real samples with satisfactory recoveries. Moreover, due to its optical properties, NCDs can also be applied to biological imaging for Ag+ sensing, which widens the horizons for the functional applications of NCDs.

YingTe Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. RongRong Wu: Visualization, Data curation. YuanYuan Zhang: Visualization, Data curation. SiRong Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yong Zhang: Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Health Commission (Grant No. 2020TD14).
